coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots in N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide assisted cellulose: A fluorescence sensitive approach for ex vivo glucose monitoring in human serum

coredata.dc:description: Bioactive functional materials have been focused recently because of their superior properties due to structural orientations. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) encapsulated chemically modified carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) nanocomposites are designed for non-enzymatic ex vivo glucose sensing by a low-cost green technique. The N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) assisted Steglich esterification between CMC and glucose is responsible for the fluorescence “Turn ON-OFF” mechanism behind glucose detection. The in vitro sensing study derived a limit of detection (LOD) of 7 nM for glucose covering two linear ranges from 0 to 0.06 mM (R2 = 0.9346) and 1.28 to 61.44 mM (R2 = 0.9704). The sensing system maintained excellent stability under extreme ionic strength and pH conditions and the high selectivity is successfully studied against interferents such as homologous sugars, amino acids, cations, and anions. Clinical potential of the proposed sensor is validated through direct testing in human blood samples against commercial glucometer. The sensor proved its reliability with recovery rates of 100 ± 5 % confirming its promising application in glucose monitoring towards effective diabetes management.

(e) Average particle size of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite (histogram). (f) SAED pattern with measurements of grain size. (g) Determination of lattice frindges and their frindge width computation. (h) First fourier transform (FFT) images and (i) Inverse FFT images. HRTEM images of CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu nanocomposite at (j,k) 20 nm, and (l) 10 nm magnification.

FTIR spectra of (a) CQD; (b) CMC/DCC composite, CQD/CMC/DCC, and CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu nanocomposites. (c) XRD plot of CQD. (d) Calculation of d-spacing values and crystallite size of CQDs using 2θ and peak width values from the XRD plot.
XPS analysis of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite (a) XPS survey spectrum; (b) C1s spectra; (c) O1s spectra; (d) N1s spectra.

Zeta potential distribution of (a) CQD (b) CMC/DCC (c) CQD/CMC/DCC (d) CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu and (e) Pictorial representation of positively charged CMC/DCC, neutral CQD/CMC/DCC, and negatively charged CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu nanocomposite. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) size distribution of (f) CQD (g) CMC/DCC (h) CQD/CMC/DCC (i) CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu and (j) Comparative representation of DLS size distribution of CMC/DCC, CQD/CMC/DCC, and CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu.

(a) Bar graph showing fluorescence emission intensities of CQD/CMC/DCC for glucose detection in existence of interfering sugars such as fructose, galactose, mannose, arabinose, and xylose. (b) Bar graph demonstrating the selective sensing ability of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite against various amino acids (including L-cysteine, lysine, tyrosine, glycine, glutamic acid, tryptophan, histidine, arginine, serine, and threonine), urea, uric acid, as well as cations (such as Na+, K+, Ca2+) and anion (Cl−). Error bars represent the standard deviation (n = 5) of five replicates.

(a) The fluorescence emission spectra of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite at various glucose concentrations, from 0 mM to 61.44 mM. (b) Calibration curve for glucose detection by CQD/CMC/DCC sensing system at glucose concentrations ranging from 0 to 61.44 mM; (inset,b) zoomed in calibration curve. Linear correlation between the fluorescence intensity and glucose concentration ranging from (c) 0 to 0.06 mM; and (d) 1.28 to 61.44 mM. Stern-Volmer fluorescence emission plot of F0/F versus glucose concentration (mM) at linear range (e) 0 to 0.06 mM; and (f) 1.28 to 61.44 mM. Error bars represent the standard deviation (n = 5) of five replicates.

(a,b) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite after addition of blood serums; (inset, a) Control (no serum) fluorescence spectrum. (c) Glucose concentration data of fifteen blood specimens determined by the suggested methodology. (d) Bar graph displays the comparative analysis of data obtained for blood glucose levels by suggested approach and commercially available glucometer.
Diagrammatic illustration of the experimental procedure for the quantification of blood glucose level by the suggested mechanism in comparison with the commercially available glucometer.

(a) Mechanistic representation of:(Step-I) the interaction of CMC with DCC; and (Step-II) encapsulation of CQDs inside CMC/DCC matrix. (b) Mechanistic representation of: (Step-III)the interaction of glucose with CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite; and (Step-IV) destabilization of CQD followed by agglomeration.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots in N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide assisted cellulose: A fluorescence sensitive approach for ex vivo glucose monitoring in human serum

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology, Burla, Sambalpur 768018, Odisha, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Veer Surendra Sai University of Technology, Burla, Sambalpur, 768018, Odisha, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bioactive functional materials have been focused recently because of their superior properties due to structural orientations. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) encapsulated chemically modified carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) nanocomposites are designed for non-enzymatic ex vivo glucose sensing by a low-cost green technique. The N,N′-Dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC) assisted Steglich esterification between CMC and glucose is responsible for the fluorescence “Turn ON-OFF” mechanism behind glucose detection. The in vitro sensing study derived a limit of detection (LOD) of 7 nM for glucose covering two linear ranges from 0 to 0.06 mM (R2 = 0.9346) and 1.28 to 61.44 mM (R2 = 0.9704). The sensing system maintained excellent stability under extreme ionic strength and pH conditions and the high selectivity is successfully studied against interferents such as homologous sugars, amino acids, cations, and anions. Clinical potential of the proposed sensor is validated through direct testing in human blood samples against commercial glucometer. The sensor proved its reliability with recovery rates of 100 ± 5 % confirming its promising application in glucose monitoring towards effective diabetes management.

Ex vivo glucose detection in real human blood serums by carbon quantum dots imprinted carboxymethyl cellulose nanocomposite assisted with N,N′-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide].
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Functional materials are integral to biosensing due to their precise interaction with biological systems, offering high sensitivity, selectivity, and stability for accurate biomolecule detection. Functional nanomaterials improve biosensors by amplifying electrochemical signals, maintaining biomolecule activity over time, and utilizing unique plasmonic and optical properties [1]. Particularly, in glucose sensing, these materials are essential in creating reliable sensors crucial for effective management of diabetes. Diabetes Mellitus has emerged as a significant and persistent metabolic disorder, characterized primarily by elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia) and symptoms such as polyuria, polydipsia, and polyphagia [2]. Prolonged hyperglycemia can lead to serious complications including diabetic nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy, and cataracts [3]. Thus, advancing the design and commercial development of glucose sensing systems is imperative to enhance therapeutic effectiveness, alleviate symptoms, and mitigate diabetes-related complications [4]. The major challenges in designing ideal glucose sensors include ensuring they are selective, fast, reliable, cost-effective, reproducible, long-lasting, non-invasive, require no calibration, and offer real-time continuous monitoring [5]. Various approaches have been utilized for this purpose, including electrochemical techniques like potentiometry, voltammetry, and impedance-based methods, as well as optical techniques such as colorimetric, fluorescence, SPR and SERS. Fluorescence sensing, in particular, typically relies on mechanisms like “Turn ON-OFF,” “Turn OFF-ON,” quenching, and signal increment [6]. In this experimental study, fluorescence “Turn ON-OFF” technique is considered to detect glucose, employing DCC-assisted CMC nanocomposites integrated with carbon quantum dots (CQD/CMC/DCC).

Despite the widespread use of glucometers for home glucose monitoring, which involves electrochemical techniques requiring a blood drop on a test strip, this method has several limitations, including invasiveness, short shelf-life, and high costs. Not only it increases the risk of infection, but also causes stress and pain due to the frequent blood extraction from patient [7]. These conventional standard glucose meters often leave patients at risk of experiencing significant glycemic fluctuations between tests [8]. To overcome these issues, fluorescence-based glucose sensors offer a cost-effective solution for real-time, continuous, label-free glucose monitoring, with benefits such as high stability, sensitivity, selectivity, non-invasiveness, ease of use, and resistance to electrochemical interferences [9,10]. Fluorescence sensing operates on the principle that a fluorophore absorbs energy at one wavelength and then emits light at a specific, different wavelength within the sensing system [11]. The interaction between the fluorophore and glucose is concentration-dependent and occurs through different mechanisms that involve the functional groups of glucose, namely alcohol, ester (present as ring form), and aldehyde (exist as linear configuration) [12]. In this study, CQDs are utilized as exceptional fluorescence probes, distinguished by their high luminescence, non-toxicity, environmental friendliness, cost-effectiveness, ease of preparation, excellent optical characteristics, good dispersibility, biocompatibility, and chemical stability [13,14]. CQDs are a new type of metal-free quantum dots, usually lower than 10 nm in size, which can display either a crystalline or amorphous structure [15]. Their outstanding size dependency behaviour allow for precise tuning of their emission wavelength, covering nearly the whole visible spectrum [16]. In CQDs, the band gap transition of conjugated π-domains in the intact carbon core is considered the primary intrinsic photoluminescence centre. Again, CQDs feature both sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbons, as well as surface defects like oxygen-containing functional groups, which are the centres for exciton capture, resulting in fluorescence [17]. In this study, arrowhead (Syngonium podophyllum) plant leaves are used as carbon precursors for CQD preparation, chosen for their medicinal properties, including antifungal, antibacterial, antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, analgesic, cytotoxic, anticancer, air-purifying, and antihypertensive effects. Moreover, CQDs obtained from medicinal plants exhibit better photostability, photoluminescence, non-toxicity, and water solubility [18].

In the development of fluorescence sensing platforms, nanocomposites based on polymers and biopolymers play a vital role, offering economical, high selectivity, sensitivity, and stability through the efficient integration of nanoscaled materials with polymer matrices. Polysaccharides like chitosan, cellulose, dextran, and alginate are especially notable for their renewable, non-toxic, biocompatible, and biodegradable properties, making them ideal for the engineering of nanocomposite-based glucose sensing systems [6]. In this work, CMC, a water soluble polysaccharide derived from cellulose via the Williamson etherification process, is adopted. Structurally, CMC consists of β-ᴅ-glucose and β-ᴅ-glucopyranose 2-O-(carboxymethyl)-monosodium salt units connected by β-1,4-glycosidic bonds, with carboxymethyl groups (-CH2-COOH) enhancing its interfacial characteristics [19]. CMC is recognized as one of the most potential cellulose derivatives because of its unique surface characteristics, tunable hydrophilicity, mechanical strength, viscosity, availability of starting materials, cost-effective fabrication method, biocompatibility, and biodegradability [20]. Among all naturally occurring biopolymers, cellulose is particularly favoured in sensing and biosensing applications especially for its high reinforcing capability and strength [21]. The hydroxyl groups in cellulose act as anchor sites for stabilizing and reducing nanoparticles, while interparticle hydrogen bonding creates a porous network structure that inhibits nanoparticle aggregation and migration [22]. These excellent properties have made CMC a popular choice for designing efficient biosensing devices. Various platforms for glucose sensing based on cellulose nanocomposites have been developed using electrochemical and optical techniques with enzymatic and non-enzymatic approaches. The incorporation of CQDs with CMC significantly alter fluorescence properties, making them suitable for sensing applications. In the present work, the CMC is again assisted with DCC to activate the esterification reaction which forms the basic mechanism of glucose sensing behind our proposed fluorescence based sensing system.

DCC is an organic component with the chemical formula (C6H11N)2C, which is a waxy white solid with sweet odor [23] and is a versatile biochemical reagent that finds applications in life sciences research. DCC acts as a dehydrating agent in the fabrication of amides, ketones, and nitriles. It facilitates reactions involving carboxylic acid groups and acid anhydrides by serving as a sacrificial activator, enabling effective alteration with a relatively small excess of reagent. Despite its infrequent use in polymer modification reactions, DCC is well-established in peptide and protein chemistry as a condensation agent for combining amines and carboxylic acids. In addition to forming amide linkages, being a condensation agent, DCC also serves as a suitable chemical component during the esterification reaction between the carboxylic groups and hydroxyl groups of alcohols at room temperature [24]. For instance, in the Steglich esterification, DCC enables the esterification of alcohols, including tertiary alcohols, using a carboxylic acid in the availability of a catalytic amount of DMAP [25]. Besides, one of the main advantages of DCC is its low volatility, making it easy to recover from reaction mixtures [26].

In this study, the focus is on CMC-based nanocomposites for glucose sensing, an area where research is still limited and primarily based on fluorescence and electrochemical techniques. For instance, Uzunçar et al. [27] developed a peroxide-selective electrode using CMC/polypyrrole and Prussian blue nanoparticles for amperometric detection of glucose. Shen et al. [28] fabricated a hydrogel having self-healing characteristic by the help of carboxymethyl chitosan and oxidized CMC, incorporating fluorescent bioprobes like gold nanoclusters and glucose oxidase for glucose detection. Similarly, Liang et al. [29] created fluorescent CMC polymer dots for glucose sensing having limit of detection (LOD) value of 52 μM. This paper newly introduced DCC into glucose sensing, whereas the incorporation of CQDs with CMC is previously carried out for different applications like food packaging [30], and drug delivery [31], but there have been no reports on their use in glucose sensing. Thus, the design of CQD-imprinted CMC as a substrate for glucose sensing and the use of chemically modified CMC with DCC for monitoring glucose are novel aspects of this study. Additionally, the exclusion of expensive enzymes allows for a cost-effective and straightforward fabrication process, enhancing the stability of the sensing platform. The scrutinization of glucose concentration levels in human blood serum as ex vivo analysis is the prime originality of this work. The enhancement in sensitivity, stability, and selectivity through the implementation of functional materials in the present sensing analysis has clearly demonstrated its ability to address key challenges in the biosensing field.

The hypothesis of this study is that the fluorescence intensity of the CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite will decrease monotonically with increasing glucose concentration due to fluorescence “Turn ON-OFF” mechanism induced by Steglich esterification reaction. This decrease in fluorescence intensity can be described by a linear relationship ‘y = mx + c’, where the fluorescence intensity (y) is inversely proportional to the concentration of glucose (x). This linear equation will enable the quantification of glucose concentration in actual human blood specimens according to the reduced fluorescence intensity measured after interaction with the nanocomposite.

The details of experimental procedures are grouped into three distinct subsections viz. synthesis, characterization and glucose sensing and given in the supplementary Section S1, S2 and S3, respectively. The materials and reagents taken for this experiment are given in the supplementary Section S1.1. CQDs were synthesized from Arrowhead (Syngonium podophyllum) leaves via a carbonization method described comprehensively in the Section S1.2. Subsequently, CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite were created through a simple solution mixing technique, by functionalizing CMC with DCC as outlined in the Section S1.3. The CQDs, CMC/DCC composite, and CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite with and without glucose were characterized by different methods, including HRTEM, optical microscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, XRD, XPS, DLS, and zeta potential measurements, detailed in the Section S2. After successful preparation and characterization of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite, they are taken for the glucose sensing study, stability and selectivity analysis, followed by glucose level quantification in actual human blood samples. The complete experimental workflow, from the synthesis of CQDs to their application in glucose detection in human blood serum, is schematically depicted in the Scheme S3.

Present work suggested a glucose sensing system using CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite through fluorescence enhancing and quenching mechanisms, which primarily transpire in two steps. The process begins with the assessment of emission intensity of the prepared CMC/DCC solution with the help of a quartz cuvette at 350 nm. Followed by incorporation of the sensing probe, here CQD, in a 1:1 ratio to the CMC/DCC solution which enhances the CQD/CMC/DCC solution's fluorescence intensity. In the subsequent phase of detection, the introduction of glucose to this composite solution effectively diminishes the fluorescence intensity. The assessment of fluorescence intensity is likewise performed for CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite together with the sequential introduction of glucose at varying concentrations which ultimately leading to the finding that incremental augmentation of glucose concentration gradually subsides the emission intensity. The Scheme S2(b) diagrammatically illustrates the “excitation-quenching mechanism” or “turn ON-OFF mechanism” of the proposed fluorescence detection system, supported by the enhancement in the intensity of fluorescence peak with the incorporation of CQD and the quenching that occurs upon the inclusion of glucose.

A biosensing system's capacity to precisely identify a target molecule in the midst of various biological system interferences is essential. Therefore, interference test is necessary to detect whether the presence of varieties of interfering agents in the mixture modifies the accurate value of the result for an analyte. Here, in this case the interfering test for glucose detection was done by analysing the fluorescence emission spectra of CQD/CMC/DCC sensing platform with assorted sugars like fructose, galactose, mannose, xylose, arabinose, having steady level of solution concentrations aligned with that of the glucose solution of 0.01 mM and volume ratio of CQD/CMC: Sugar is 2: 2 in the course of fluorescence measurement. The sugars are individually added into the CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite pre-loaded with glucose and the fluorescence spectra are measured.

Selectivity and specificity of the suggested fluorescence method for detecting glucose was scrutinized through analysis of the intensities variation of fluorescence emission spectra of the CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposites after the introduction of various amino acids including histidine, glutamic acid, arginine, lysine, glycine, tryptophan, tyrosine, L-cysteine, serine, and threonine as well as certain cations such as Na+, Ca2+, and K+, and anion such as Cl¯. Throughout, the fluorescence measurements, the concentrations of all interfering agent solutions maintained same with the glucose solution of 0.2 mM and volume ratio of CQD/CMC: glucose: interfering agents is 2: 1: 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement and analysis of glucose levels and diabetes status in human blood
ce:section.ce:para: Fifteen blood samples were obtained from the nearby hospital's pathology department. Initially, the glucose content of the blood samples was measured using a commercially available glucometer and the readings are documented. Subsequently, the blood samples were centrifuged for 40 min at 10,000 rpm to isolate the blood serums, and the supernatant blood serums for each blood sample were collected. Following required dilution, the resultant blood serums were introduced into the reaction platform which comprises of CMC/DCC/CQD nanocomposite solution, and taken for fluorescence spectra measurements. In this study, the proposed method is compared with the widely recognized commercial ACCU-CHEK® Instant S Blood Glucose Meter. The Accu-Chek Instant S system has a top score of 10/10 for its consistent results compared to laboratory readings and adheres to ISO 15197:2013/EN ISO 15197:2015 standards. Scheme 1 outlines the process for determining the glucose concentration in actual human blood samples using the suggested fluorescence technique in comparison to the commercial glucometer.

Mechanistic approach behind glucose sensing by CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite, in conjunction with the suggested fluorescent approach is explained by a total of four steps (Scheme 2 ). The stabilization of CQDs with CMC/DCC matrix and interaction of glucose with CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite is illustrated in the Scheme 2(a) and Scheme 2(b), respectively. In the first step, DCC interacts with CMC, the stabilizing agent which results in the protonation of DCC at nitrogen atom followed by nucleophilic interaction between the negatively charged carboxylate groups of CMC with the electrophilic carbon centre at DCC forming an O-acylisourea derivative of CMC, which under further protonation produces a CMC derivative with positive surface charge, in similar pathway as Steglich Esterification reaction [32]. The second step involves the encapsulation of CQDs inside the CMC/DCC matrix, in which the sensing probe CQD is highly stabilized by the electrostatic interaction present between the negatively charged surface of CQD with the positively charged surface of O-acylisourea derivative of CMC. As a result of encasing the extremely stable fluorescent probe CQD, the fluorescence intensity increased, as mentioned in the mechanism given by Mansuret al. [33] in 2017. But in the ensuing phase, when glucose interacted with the CQD/CMC/DCC framework, the -OH group of glucose undergoes esterification process by nucleophilic interaction with carbonyl carbon present in the carboxylate part of O-acylisourea derivative of CMC thereby neutralize the CMC surface with the formation of an ester according to Steglich Esterification reaction [32]. As a result of this neutralization in CMC surface, the electrostatic interaction between CMC and CQD surface get depleted resulting in the destabilization of CQDs in the CQD/CMC/DCC matrix thereby decreasing fluorescence intensity.

The HRTEM images of as-synthesized CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite at four different magnifications i.e., 200 nm, 100 nm, 50 nm, and 20 nm are given in the Fig. 1 (a-d), in which the presence of homogeneous and highly monodispersed CQDs are clearly evident. This uniform and individual dispersion of CQDs in the nanocomposite is due to the excellent stabilization of CQDs in the CMC/DCC matrix by electrostatic interactions. The average particle size of CQDs within the CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite is determined to be 2.9 nm, with a standard deviation (SD) of 0.806 nm based on N = 26 measurements. The polydispersity index (P) value of 0.28 is calculated from the size distribution histogram presented in the Fig. 1(e). The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite is analysed with grain sizes ranging from 1.1 Å to 2.9 Å as shown in the Fig. 1(f). The HRTEM image, magnified to 5 nm, reveals the presence of lattice fringes, indicating that the crystalline CQD possesses single crystalline phase (Fig. 1(g)). Widths of 0.09 nm, 0.1 nm, 0.12 nm, and 0.14 nm are computed for the observed in-plane lattice fringes. Specifically, the 0.14 nm lattice spacing relates with the [102] facet of graphitic carbon [34], suggesting a highly graphitized structure in the synthesized CQDs. The Fig. 1(h) and (i) display the fast Fourier transform (FFT) images and the inverse FFT images of these focused regions, respectively. The electron microscopy analysis of the CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite provides a clear understanding of the size, distribution, and interaction of CQDs within the composite matrix. However, after interaction with glucose, as shown in Fig. 1(j-l) at 20 nm and 10 nm magnifications, distinct agglomerated regions are visible. This observation supports the proposed mechanism, stating when glucose gets attached within the CMC fibre network results in destabilization and agglomeration of CQDs.

Optical microscopy investigations commonly employ a configuration of lenses and visible light to produce magnified representations of diminutive subjects. Here, the optical microscopy images of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite are studied with and without glucose at different magnifications (Fig. S1). These images are composed of dark spots which are due to the dispersion of CQDs and CMC fibres are clearly distinguishable at 500 μm magnification in the Fig. S1(a), with some networking structure which stems from entanglement of CMC polymeric chain around CQDs, thereby adds augmented stability to CQDs. The magnified image of CQD/CMC/DCC at 200 μm is represented in the Fig. S1(b), in which CMC fibres are more prominent containing CQDs within the networks. The optical microscopy images of CQD/CMC/DCC with glucose at 500 μm and 200 μm are represented in the Fig. S1(c) and Fig. S1(d), respectively, display noticeable agglomerated areas. These observations support the findings from the HRTEM analysis.

UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy are extensively investigated to establish the photophysical characteristics of our sensing probe CQD (Fig. S2). The UV–Vis spectrum of CQDs depicted in the Fig. S2(a) reveals two distinct peaks which are observed at 274 and 327 nm. These peaks correspond to the π–π* electronic transition occurs due to the presence of sp2 domains and the n–π* transition arises owing to the multiple conjugation associated with the CO groups available in the CQDs, respectively, indicating the formation of functionalized CQDs. Similar maxima have been observed in previous studies for untreated CQDs. For instance, carbon dots synthesized from PEG, ethylenediamine, and citric acid have exhibited comparable absorption characteristics [35].

Excited state energy movement depends on the excitation wavelength and may cause variations in the fluorescence spectrum. These changes, which are also called “red-edge effects”, are explained by the distribution of fluorophores' excited states along with their energy of interaction with the milieu and the leisurely dielectric relaxation rate of that environment. Essentially, fluorescence spectra at lower temperatures are more likely to move towards higher wavelength values when the excitation takes place near the absorption spectrum's protracted wavelength area, also known as “the red edge” [36]. Further, there are two laws i.e., Kasha's rule and Vavilov's law those have different perspectives about the fluorescence emission. Kasha's rule states that regardless of the excitation energy or wavelength, fluorescence emission always originates from the lowest electronic excited state whereas Vavilov's law states that the energy of fluorescence emission spectra is independent of excitation wavelength [37,38]. However, these effects arise from the way they operate under certain circumstances i.e., from how the ensemble of excited molecules interacts with molecules around them, rather than from a breach of fundamental rules. Here, the Fig. S2(b) shows the as-prepared CQD's excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission spectra ranging from 230 to 560 nm and Fig. S2(c) represents the alteration in intensities of fluorescence emission spectra. The spectra display fragmented rope net like sequencing with the emission intensities increasing from 230 nm to 340 nm, then progressively decreasing from 340 nm to 560 nm. This observation can be attributed to variations in CQD size, the arrangement of oxygen comprising functional groups, various surface conditions, or the existence of distinct energy confined on CQD surfaces [39]. Excitonic transitions between the lowermost broken conduction band and the highest broken valence band could account for the highest emission intensity showed at 340 nm [40] and the reduction in intensity is consistent with the π-π* electronic transition testified from the UV–Vis assessment. Moreover, because of the quantum confinement effect, using higher excitation wavelengths excite larger quantum dots with narrower band gaps. This leads to a noticeable bathochromic shift as the excitation wavelength increases [41]. This excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence emission nature indicates that customizable fluorescent light emission is possible with the as-prepared CQDs. The explanations provided distinctly illustrate that CQDs developed have unique multi-coloured photophysical attributes, along with their adaptable emissive capabilities. Fig. S2(d) represents the CIE chromaticity coordinates of the response of CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.

FTIR analysis involves the investigation of functional characteristics and potential covalent interactions between CQD, CMC, DCC, and glucose as well as their mixtures. The significant absorption peak observed at 3336 cm−1 in the FTIR spectra of CQD is associated with the stretching of free OH bonds, whereas the distinct peak at 1637 cm−1 depicts the stretching of >C=O bonds within the carboxyl groups (Fig. 2(a)). It is likely that the existence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in CQD can be attributed to its synthesis in an aqueous environment. However, this functionalized CQD exhibits a strong attraction towards the accessible functional groups present in CMC/DCC matrix. The molecular interactions among individual components in composites also in the case of with and without glucose are characterized by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2(b)). The covalent and non-covalent interactions are identified between the functional groups exist in CMC, DCC, and glucose along with CQD. From the mechanism it is found that DCC is protonated by treatment with CMC and form a covalent bond for which the >C=O stretching frequency of the ester appeared at 1718 cm−1. Furthermore, both OH and NH stretching are responsible for the distinctive peak at 3308 cm−1. Moreover, >C=O of carboxyl groups and CN stretching are the reason behind the transmittance peak at 1636 cm−1 and 1326 cm−1, respectively. The peaks found at 1192 cm−1 and 1084 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching frequency of the CO of ether and 1° alcohol, respectively. When, CQD is incorporated with CMC/DCC, there is no appreciable change in FTIR peaks indicates the appearance of non-covalent electrostatic interaction of CQD in CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite. However, slight change in the wave numbers of different FTIR peaks may be due to change in orientation of CMC/DCC with CQD. The addition of glucose gives a sharper peak at 1718 cm−1 owing to both the availability of aldehyde group and ester linkage due to the esterification reaction between CMC and glucose. Therefore, these results from FTIR analysis strongly support the proposed mechanism presented in the Scheme 2. Further, a weak but distinct broad band, centered around ~2100 cm−1, is observed in the FTIR spectra of all samples. This band is attributed to the water combination band, likely due to the aqueous medium used during sample preparation [42]. Bond vibrations and wavenumbers of CQD, CMC/DCC, CQD/CMC/DCC, and CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu in FTIR spectra are compiled in the Table S1.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is effectively utilized for obtaining information regarding the crystallite size, crystalline phases, and crystallinity of CQDs. The XRD pattern of CQDs, presented in the Fig. 2(c), exhibits multiple peaks, indicative of the presence of CQDs along with impurities in the sample. The characteristic XRD peaks are observed at 2θ values of 25.51°, 28.3°, 29.72°, 33.29°, 34.04°, and 40.59°. The primary peaks for the CQDs are noted at 2θ = 29.72° and 40.59°, attributed to the existence of graphitic carbon. Notably, the sharp peak at 29.72° (with a diffraction angle greater than 21.1°) suggests the presence of coarse carbon nanomaterials in the CQDs [43]. The Fig. 2(d) provides the 2θ values, full width at half maximum (FWHM), d-spacing, and crystallite sizes (τ) of the respective CQD peaks, calculated using Bragg's law and the Debye-Scherrer equation. The interplanar spacing (d) for the peak at 29.72° is determined to be 0.3 nm, which is closely related with the reported value for graphitic carbon (0.33 nm), indicating that the CQDs possess a crystallinity level comparable to that of graphitic carbon [44]. The average crystallite size of CQDs is computed as 18.6 nm, with a crystallinity of 57.7 %.

XPS is carried out to analyse the elemental composition, binding energy, and surface features of the CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite, as depicted in the Fig. 3 . The comprehensive survey spectrum in the Fig. 3(a) indicates the presence of carbon (C1s), oxygen (O1s), and nitrogen (N1s) core levels, which are found around 281 eV, 529.8 eV, and 398.3 eV, respectively. High-resolution spectra for C1s, O1s, and N1s are shown in the Fig. 3(b-d). In the Fig. 3(b), the C1s peak is resolved into three separate components at 282.8 eV, 290.5 eV, and 293.3 eV. The intense peak at 282.8 eV corresponds to the C-C/C=C bond, while the less intense peak at 293.3 eV is associated with the CN bond. The peak at 290.5 eV is attributed to C-O/C=O bonds. The Fig. 3(c) shows O1s peak at 529.8 eV, indicating -C-OH/C–O/C=O bonds. Additionally, the high-resolution N1s spectrum in the Fig. 3(d) displays two prominent peaks at 403.6 eV and 397.2 eV, corresponding to the NH and CN bonds, respectively. These findings confirm the successful functionalization of CMC with DCC and the significant encapsulation of CQDs. Similar peaks have been reported in previous XPS studies of CQD [45] and sulfated-CMC/multiwalled carbon nanotubes [46].

The colloidal stability of CQD is analysed using the zeta potential plot (Fig. 4(a)). The results revealed two peaks at −21.0 mV and − 7.74 mV, with an average zeta potential of −15.3 mV and a fluctuation of 8.70 mV. These negative values of zeta potential indicate the presence of functional groups such as OH− and COO− those having negatively charged moiety, as accorded by the FTIR study shown in the Fig. 2(a). This surface negative charge on CQD supports its electrostatic interaction with the CMC/DCC matrix, as illustrated in the Scheme 2(a). Additionally, the negative charge contributes to the magnificent dispersion of CQD in water medium. This negative zeta potential data thus agrees with the stable colloidal structure of CQD [47,48]. To evidence this claim, the CQD is at stored room temperature for about eight months, during which no turbidity is observed. This indicates that the prepared CQD possesses a high level of colloidal stability, making it suitable for long-term use. The zeta potential curves of CMC/DCC, CQD/CMC/DCC, and CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu are given in the Fig. 4(b-d). The CMC/DCC alone (Fig. 4(b)) showed a positive zeta potential of 0.424 mV with a zeta deviation of 2.29 mV. When combined with CQD (Fig. 4(c)), almost neutral zeta potential of −0.0477 mV is observed with zeta deviation of 2.94 mV, suggesting the neutralization of composite due to the electrostatic interaction between CMC/DCC matrix and CQD. Upon the addition of glucose (Fig. 4(d)), the zeta potential shifts to −0.615 mV, with zeta deviation of 2.28 mV indicating an increase in negative charge due to removal of negatively charged CQD from the matrix owing to the esterification reaction of glucose with CMC/DCC (Fig. 4(e)). Further, the low zeta potential value indicates aggregation of particles within the nanocomposite.

The size distribution profile of particles in CQD, CMC/DCC, CQD/CMC/DCC and CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu in colloidal suspension is investigated employing DLS approach. The produced CQDs have an average size of 2.01 nm and a very low polydispersity index (PdI) of 0.008 (Fig. 4(f)), indicating the formation of highly monodispersed CQDs. This result is consistent with the average particle size obtained from the size distribution histogram in the HRTEM analysis, as shown in the Fig. 1(e). The typical grain size for pristine CMC/DCC is revealed to be 784.2 d.nm with a high PdI value of 0.763 (Fig. 4(g)). When the sensing probe CQD is imprinted within the CMC/DCC matrix, there is diminishing in the grain size of the nanocomposite noticed to 551.3 d.nm with a low PdI of 0.674 as compared to CMC/DCC composite (Fig. 4(h)), which could be owing to the stability of the nanocomposite by electrostatic interaction of CQD with the CMC/DCC matrix. Similar observations have been reported previously, where the inclusion of quantum dots led to a minimization in the hydrodynamic diameters of the microgels, where quantum dots were acting as local cross-linkers via electrostatic association [49]. Due to glucose interaction, CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu nanocomposite face an augmentation in grain size up to 637.4 d.nm with a PdI of 0.817 (Fig. 4(i)), emanating from the esterification of glucose molecule with the CQD/CMC/DCC matrix causing the abolishment of CQDs from the surface followed by agglomeration. Correspondingly the Fig. 4(j) presents the comparative DLS size distribution curve for all composites.

To achieve optimal glucose sensing with the CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite, the individual components are systematically optimized as detailed in the Section S3.1 and Section S3.2. The study identified the optimal composition as 0.2 wt% of CMC, 0.01 M concentration of DCC, and CQD:CMC at a 1.5:0.5 volume in the nanocomposite. The rationale for selecting DCC-assisted CMC nanocomposites combined with CQDs for glucose sensing is explained through the control experiment in the Section S3.3. The time-dependent fluorescence properties of the CQD/CMC/DCC composite are examined in the Section S3.4 which ensures the stability and accuracy of the proposed sensing system over time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stimuli responsiveness of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite for glucose sensing
ce:section.ce:para: When a sensing system is exposed to a complex biological environment, variables like pH and ionic strength can interfere, causing instability and inaccuracy. Therefore, the susceptibility of our proposed CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite for sensing is investigated under conditions of varying ionic strength and pH. The Fig. S5(c) illustrates the fluorescence intensities of both CQD/CMC/DCC and CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu with NaCl solution concentrations ranging from 0 to 1 M. The change in emission intensity remained negligible with increasing NaCl concentration in the CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite. In contrast, slight incremental diminishment in fluorescence intensity is noted in the presence of glucose. This decline may be due to the increased esterification rate when glucose is added to the nanocomposite at higher NaCl concentrations, leading to the destabilization of the CQD sensing probe. Quite similar observations are drawn by Wong et al. [50] where the 3-monochloropropane-1,2-diol esters trend is increased by increasing the NaCl concentration. Similarly, the Fig. S5(d) show the fluorescence emission spectra of both CQD/CMC/DCC and CQD/CMC/DCC@Glu at varying pH levels, ranging from 2 to 13. The CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite exhibits relatively stable fluorescence intensity across all pH conditions. After integrating glucose with the composite, the fluorescence intensity remains stable in acidic conditions but gradually increases in basic pH conditions. This increase may be attributed to the decreased rate of esterification at higher pH levels, reducing the reactivity of glucose with the composite and thereby maintaining the stability of the sensing probe. Cui et al. [51] also in their study suggested that lower pH promotes esterification. However, there is no noticeable change in emission intensity within the pH range of 6.5 to 7.5, which includes the typical blood pH range of 7.35 to 7.45 (average 7.40). This confirms that our proposed CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite is stable and suitable to detect glucose in actual human blood samples.

For a biosensor, anti-interference capability is crucial. The interfering test is carried out to assess the sensor's performance during the availability of common interfering components in glucose detection such as fructose, galactose, mannose, arabinose, and xylose (Fig. 5(a)). The fluorescence emission results indicate that CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite maintains consistent emission intensity even with the presence of these interfering sugars which demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity remain unaffected by these sugars, confirming the excellent selectivity and sensing ability of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite for glucose detection.

Selectivity in sensors is essential as it enables each device to uniquely identify a sample by interacting differently with specific target analytes. This is particularly important in complex biological environments where numerous interfering substances are present. We have investigated the selectivity of our proposed CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite for detecting glucose in the presence of various potential interferents, including amino acids (L-cysteine, lysine, tyrosine, glycine, glutamic acid, tryptophan, histidine, arginine, serine, and threonine), urea, uric acid, cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+), and anion (Cl−). The results obtained from Fig. 5(b) show that the fluorescence intensity significantly decreased for glucose compared to other substances, indicating the high specificity of the CQD/CMC/DCC system for glucose detection. This high specificity is primarily due to the absence of hydroxyl groups in the other interfering substances, preventing the esterification reaction that would otherwise destabilize the sensing probe and reduce fluorescence. Again, the amine group of amino acids cannot undergo nucleophilic reactions with CMC, unlike the hydroxyl groups of glucose. Amino acids predominantly exist in their zwitterionic form in aqueous solutions [52], where the protonation of the amino group (-NH3 +) and deprotonation of the carboxyl group (-COO−) occurs. Therefore, the -NH2 group is not freely available as nucleophile to interact with the carboxylate part of CMC. However, amino acids like L-cysteine contain free thiol group, while tyrosine, serine, and threonine have free hydroxyl groups, which cause a minimal reduction in fluorescence intensity due to the lower degree esterification reaction. This negligible response of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite based sensing system to other interfering species concludes the high selectivity of the suggested methodology for glucose detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LOD and linear range of the CQD/CMC/DCC sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The results obtained from fluorescence analysis of the CMC/DCC/CQD at various glucose concentrations, ranging from 0 to 61.44 mM, are displayed in the Fig. 6(a). As glucose concentration increases, a steady downfall is seen for emission intensity. The calibration curve is demonstrated in Fig. 6(b), with a zoomed view (inset, Fig. 6(b)). Additionally, Fig. 6(c) and Fig. 6(d) illustrates the calibration plots with two linear relationships between glucose concentrations and emission intensity. The two regression equations are y = −1120.7× + 449.4 (R2 = 0.9346) and y = −0.665× + 347.81 (R2 = 0.9704), indicating the linear ranges of the glucose detection technique as 0 to 0.06 mM and 1.28 to 61.44 mM, respectively. The two LODs are determined to be 7 nM and 12 μM with help of formula LOD = 3σ/m, where ‘σ’ is the standard deviation and ‘m’ is the slope of the calibration plots presented in the Fig. 6(c) and Fig. 6(d). The Stern–Volmer plots for two linear ranges are generated as displayed in the Fig. 6(e) and Fig. 6(f). In these plots, the relative quenching efficiency (F0/F) follows a second-order polynomial relationship. The equations for the two linear ranges are y = −28.826 × 2 + 4.5459× + 1.0118 with an R2 value of 0.984 and y = −2E-05 × 2 + 0.0037× + 1.3154 with an R2 value of 0.9903. The measured linear range and LOD have superior results than those of the formerly summarised literatures for glucose detection platforms prepared from nanocomposites, as shown in the Table S2.

This study focuses on the investigation, development, and validation of the CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite for diabetes management. To achieve this goal, the nanocomposite is implemented to sense blood glucose levels in actual human blood samples. The Fig. 7 (a,b) show the fluorescence emission spectra of the CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite following the inclusion of blood serums. The inset of Fig. 7(a) is the fluorescence spectra of CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite without addition of blood serum denoted as control. The fluorescence intensities of all fifteen blood serums reduced compared to the CQD/CMC/DCC nanocomposite alone, indicating “Turn ON-OFF” of fluorescence spectra as desired. These fluorescence intensities are recorded from which the glucose concentrations in each of the fifteen human blood serums are calculated. The results are presented in the Fig. 7(c), with glucose levels ranging from 3.86 to 22.98 mM in all the fifteen blood samples. For diagnosis, the normal fasting blood glucose concentration levels for adults: diabetic (126 mg/dL or above), pre-diabetic (100 to 125 mg/dL), and normal (70 to 100 mg/dL) is referenced, following to the data given by the World Health Organization's Global Health Observatory [53]. From the analysis twelve blood specimens are ascertained to be diabetic, one pre-diabetic, one with hypoglycemia, and one normal. Further, these resulted blood glucose concentration values are correlated with the glucose concentrations measured by the commercial blood glucometer as displayed in the Fig. 7(d). The results are shown strong similarity, with recovery percentages ranging from 96.2 % to 115.88 % and relative standard deviations (RSD) ranging from 0.79 % to 3.35 %. These findings clearly demonstrate the reliability and accuracy of the suggested blood glucose sensing technique. The slight variation in the fluorescence profile with blood serums than control is attributed to the interaction of the nanocomposites with biomolecules such as enzymes and proteins present in the blood serum. However, this interaction does not affect the overall sensing performance. Table S3 comprises the study comparing the blood glucose levels determined by our method to those from the commercially available glucometer, including recovery percentages, RSD, and diagnostic outcomes.

The CQDs as fluorescent probes are synthesized from arrowhead plant leaves via carbonization, through an eco-friendly and sustainable method. Then they are successfully combined with CMC/DCC matrix resulting an effective nanocomposite system for non-enzymatic ex vivo glucose detection. CQDs along with the nanocomposites are characterized via HRTEM, XPS, FTIR, UV–Vis, XRD, DLS, and fluorescence spectroscopy ensuring their optimal sensitivity. Zeta potential measurements indicated the surface negative charge of CQD inducing effective electrostatic interactions within the CMC/DCC nanocomposite. The sensor demonstrated two LODs of 7 nM and 12 μM for glucose corresponding to two wide linear ranges 0 to 0.06 mM and 1.28 to 61.44 mM. The fluorescence stability of CQD/CMC/DCC is established under extravagant ionic strength and pH circumvents. The suitability of the suggested detection platform for practical real-world implementations is established by clinical testing in real human blood samples with satisfying recoveries between 96.2 % and 115.88 % as compared to commercially available glucometer. The study validates the hypothesis by demonstrating excellent LOD and linear range, as well as precise glucose measurement in human blood samples by highlighting the sensor's significant potential for effective real-world clinical application in diabetes monitoring. Despite these promising advantages, this study has certain limitations. These include the inability to reuse the materials after glucose measurement and appearance of errors during the handling of fluorescence instrument by human which leads to operational issues.

Swapnita Patra: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Debasis Sahoo: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Sarat K. Swain: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration.
